title: Easy Five-Ingredient Chocolate Cake for Cozy Weeknights

description: This chocolate cake is my quick go-to when I want homemade but simple. Just five ingredients, no fancy tools, and it’s ready in under an hour. Great for a last-minute cozy dessert!

pin_variations.title: Your New Favorite Easy Chocolate Cake Recipe in Under 60 Minutes
pin_variations.description: This chocolate cake is a lifesaver when you want something delicious but quick. Just five simple ingredients, no fancy tools, and ready in under an hour. Save it now and bake soon! 🍰👌 #chocolatecake #easyrecipe #weeknightdessert

introduction: I stumbled upon this chocolate cake recipe years ago when I needed a quick dessert that didn’t require a dozen ingredients or fancy equipment. It’s become my go-to for those cozy evenings when I want something homemade but don’t want to spend hours in the kitchen. What I love about this cake is how simple it is — just five pantry staples, no eggs, and it bakes up moist and tender every time. The trick I learned early on is adding vinegar and baking soda together to get that perfect rise and a light crumb, which surprised me at first but really works wonders. This cake is lightly chocolatey but not overpowering, making it a crowd-pleaser whether you eat it plain or dress it up with a scoop of ice cream. Honestly, it’s the kind of recipe that feels like a warm hug after a long day. Plus, it’s forgiving, so even if you’re a beginner, you’ll have success. I think you’ll enjoy how effortlessly it comes together and the comfort it brings with every bite.

instructions.instruction: Preheat your oven to 350°F (175°C) and grease an 8-inch round cake pan with a little oil or butter. This step ensures your cake won’t stick and will come out cleanly. Greasing the pan evenly is key—don’t forget the sides! A common mistake is to skip this and then struggle to remove the cake later. If you want, line the bottom with parchment paper for extra insurance.
instructions.doneness_cue: Pan feels evenly coated and lightly slick to the touch

instructions.instruction: In a large mixing bowl, sift together the flour, sugar, cocoa powder, and baking soda. Sifting helps eliminate lumps and aerates the dry ingredients, which leads to a lighter cake texture. Take your time here; rushing can cause uneven mixing or clumps of cocoa powder that don’t dissolve well.
instructions.doneness_cue: Dry ingredients are fully combined, smooth, and lump-free

instructions.instruction: Add the water, vegetable oil, vanilla extract, and vinegar to the dry ingredients. Stir gently with a whisk or spoon until just combined—don’t overmix, or the cake can turn dense. The vinegar reacts with baking soda to create bubbles that help the cake rise and stay fluffy.
instructions.doneness_cue: Batter is smooth and glossy with no visible streaks of flour

instructions.instruction: Pour the batter into the greased pan and smooth the top with a spatula. This ensures even baking and a nice flat surface. Avoid tapping the pan hard, as it can deflate the air bubbles you want to keep in the batter.
instructions.doneness_cue: Batter is evenly spread and level in the pan

instructions.instruction: Bake in the preheated oven for about 30 minutes. Start checking around 25 minutes by inserting a toothpick in the center—if it comes out clean or with a few moist crumbs, it’s done. Baking too long dries it out, too short leaves it gooey inside.
instructions.doneness_cue: Toothpick inserted in center comes out clean or with moist crumbs

instructions.instruction: Remove the cake from the oven and let it cool in the pan for 10 minutes. Cooling a bit allows the cake to firm up and makes it easier to remove from the pan without breaking. Resist the urge to unmold it while still hot—it’s fragile then.
instructions.doneness_cue: Cake edges start to pull away slightly from the pan

instructions.instruction: Run a knife around the edges to loosen the cake, then invert it onto a wire rack to cool completely. Cooling fully helps the crumb set and prevents it from being gummy or collapsing when sliced.
instructions.doneness_cue: Cake is completely cool to the touch and firm

instructions.instruction: Once cooled, slice and serve as is, or add a dusting of powdered sugar, a dollop of whipped cream, or your favorite ice cream. This cake is very versatile and pairs beautifully with simple toppings that don’t overpower its delicate chocolate flavor.
instructions.doneness_cue: Cake slices cleanly without crumbling excessively

instructions.instruction: Store any leftovers in an airtight container at room temperature for up to 3 days. This keeps the cake moist without drying out. If you want to keep it longer, refrigeration or freezing works well but can slightly change texture.
instructions.doneness_cue: Cake remains moist and fresh when stored properly

why_this_recipe_works.section: Why This Simple Chocolate Cake Works Every Time
why_this_recipe_works.content:
This chocolate cake relies on a few smart ingredient interactions to deliver excellent texture and flavor with minimal fuss. The combination of baking soda and vinegar is the real star here. When they mix, they create carbon dioxide bubbles that expand during baking, giving the cake a light, tender crumb despite the lack of eggs or butter. This reaction is why the cake rises so well and stays moist.

The use of vegetable oil instead of butter also helps keep the cake moist and tender. Oil remains liquid at room temperature, which means the crumb doesn’t firm up too much and retains a pleasant softness. Butter, while flavorful, can sometimes make cakes denser if not creamed properly.

Sifting the dry ingredients is more than just a formality; it aerates the flour and cocoa powder, preventing lumps and ensuring even mixing. This step contributes to the cake’s uniform crumb and prevents bitter cocoa clumps. Plus, mixing the wet and dry ingredients gently helps avoid developing too much gluten, which would make the cake tough.

Finally, baking at a moderate temperature of 350°F allows the cake to cook evenly. Too high a heat would dry out the edges before the center is done, while too low would make it dense and gummy. Watching the toothpick test closely ensures you catch it at the perfect moment for moistness and structure.

ingredient_notes.all-purpose flour: Choose a good-quality all-purpose flour for this recipe. It provides the right balance of protein to develop enough gluten for structure without making the cake tough. Avoid self-rising flour here since we rely on baking soda for leavening. Sifting the flour before mixing helps prevent lumps and aerates it for a lighter crumb.
ingredient_notes.cocoa powder: Use unsweetened cocoa powder for a rich chocolate flavor without added sugar. Natural cocoa powder is acidic and reacts well with baking soda and vinegar to help the cake rise. Avoid Dutch-processed cocoa here since its neutral pH can interfere with the leavening reaction.
ingredient_notes.baking soda: Baking soda is the primary leavening agent in this cake. It reacts with the vinegar to produce carbon dioxide bubbles that lighten the batter. Make sure your baking soda is fresh and not expired, or the cake won’t rise properly. Measure carefully to avoid a soapy taste.
ingredient_notes.vegetable oil: Vegetable oil keeps the cake moist and tender because it stays liquid at room temperature. It also has a neutral flavor so it doesn’t overpower the chocolate. You can substitute with light olive oil or melted coconut oil, but avoid strong-flavored oils that might clash.
ingredient_notes.vinegar: White vinegar reacts with baking soda to create the leavening bubbles needed for rise and tenderness. It’s important to add it fresh and not substitute with lemon juice, which can alter flavor. The vinegar taste disappears completely during baking, leaving just a soft crumb.

pro_tips.tip: Always sift your dry ingredients before mixing.
pro_tips.reason: Sifting aerates the flour and cocoa powder and removes lumps, which helps create a light, even crumb. If you skip this, you risk clumps of cocoa powder or flour pockets that can make the cake dense or unevenly flavored.

pro_tips.tip: Mix wet and dry ingredients just until combined.
pro_tips.reason: Overmixing develops gluten in the flour, making the cake tough instead of tender. Stir gently until no dry spots remain to keep the crumb soft and delicate.

pro_tips.tip: Use room temperature water and oil for best results.
pro_tips.reason: Cold liquids can cause uneven mixing and interfere with the baking soda and vinegar reaction. Room temperature ingredients blend smoothly and activate the leavening agents properly.

pro_tips.tip: Don’t skip the vinegar—it’s key to rise.
pro_tips.reason: The acid in vinegar reacts with baking soda to produce carbon dioxide bubbles that give the cake lift. Without it, the cake will be dense and flat.

pro_tips.tip: Check the cake early with a toothpick test.
pro_tips.reason: Oven temperatures vary, so start testing the cake a few minutes before the suggested time. Pulling it out at the right moment ensures moistness and prevents dryness.

pro_tips.tip: Cool the cake in the pan before removing.
pro_tips.reason: This allows the structure to set and prevents the cake from breaking apart when unmolding. Patience here pays off with a clean cake shape.

pro_tips.tip: Store leftovers in an airtight container at room temperature.
pro_tips.reason: This keeps the cake moist and fresh for several days. Refrigeration can dry it out, so only refrigerate if your kitchen is very warm.

common_mistakes_to_avoid.mistake: Skipping the vinegar or using lemon juice instead.
common_mistakes_to_avoid.why_it_happens: Some cooks forget the role of vinegar or try to substitute lemon juice, assuming the acid will behave the same.
common_mistakes_to_avoid.fix: Use white vinegar as specified to ensure the proper chemical reaction with baking soda. Lemon juice has a different acidity level and flavor that can affect rise and taste.

common_mistakes_to_avoid.why_it_happens: It’s tempting to stir thoroughly to avoid lumps, but this develops gluten and toughens the cake.
common_mistakes_to_avoid.fix: Mix gently until just combined. A few small lumps are okay; they will hydrate during baking.

common_mistakes_to_avoid.why_it_happens: Sometimes people assume flouring the pan or nothing at all is fine, leading to sticking.
common_mistakes_to_avoid.fix: Grease the pan generously with oil or butter and optionally line with parchment paper to prevent sticking.

common_mistakes_to_avoid.why_it_happens: Ovens vary and some cooks don’t preheat fully or set the wrong temp.
common_mistakes_to_avoid.fix: Always preheat to 350°F and use an oven thermometer if needed for accuracy.

common_mistakes_to_avoid.mistake: Removing the cake from the pan too soon.
common_mistakes_to_avoid.why_it_happens: Impatience leads to trying to unmold while cake is still fragile and warm.
common_mistakes_to_avoid.fix: Let it cool in the pan for at least 10 minutes to firm up and prevent breaking.

variations.name: Gluten-Free Version
variations.description: Swap the all-purpose flour for a 1:1 gluten-free baking blend. The texture will be slightly different but still moist and delicious. Make sure your blend contains xanthan gum for structure.
variations.technical_note: Gluten-free flours absorb liquids differently, so you might need to add a tablespoon more water if the batter seems too thick.

variations.name: Add Espresso for Richness
variations.description: Stir in 1 teaspoon of instant espresso powder into the water before mixing. It enhances the chocolate flavor without making it taste like coffee.
variations.technical_note: Dissolve espresso powder fully to avoid grainy texture and uneven flavor.

variations.name: Vegan Frosting Topping
variations.description: Top the cake with a simple vegan chocolate ganache made from coconut cream and dark chocolate for a creamy finish that complements the moist cake.
variations.technical_note: Heat coconut cream gently to avoid separating, then whisk with chopped chocolate until smooth.

variations.name: Add Chopped Nuts or Chocolate Chips
variations.description: Fold in 1/2 cup chopped walnuts or chocolate chips into the batter for added texture and flavor. This adds a delightful crunch or gooey bite.
variations.technical_note: Gently fold in to avoid deflating the batter and distribute mix-ins evenly.

variations.name: Orange Zest Twist
variations.description: Add 1 tablespoon fresh orange zest to the batter for a bright citrus note that pairs beautifully with chocolate.
variations.technical_note: Use only the colored part of the zest to avoid bitterness.

nutrition.note: Nutritional values are estimates and may vary based on specific ingredients used.

storage_and_reheating.room_temperature: Store the cake covered loosely with plastic wrap or in an airtight container at room temperature for up to 3 days. This keeps it moist and prevents drying out. Avoid direct sunlight or heat sources.
storage_and_reheating.refrigerator: You can refrigerate the cake in an airtight container for up to 5 days. Keep in mind refrigeration may dry the cake slightly, so bring it to room temperature before serving or warm gently in the microwave for 10-15 seconds.
storage_and_reheating.freezer: Wrap the cooled cake tightly in plastic wrap and then aluminum foil before freezing. Freeze for up to 2 months. Thaw overnight in the fridge or at room temperature. Reheat slices gently to restore softness.
storage_and_reheating.reheating: Warm slices in the microwave for 10-20 seconds or in a 300°F oven for 5-7 minutes wrapped in foil. Add a splash of milk or water if the cake feels dry to restore moisture. Avoid overheating to prevent drying.
storage_and_reheating.make_ahead_strategy: You can mix the dry ingredients ahead and store them in an airtight container for up to a week. On the day, just add the wet ingredients and bake fresh. Alternatively, bake the cake a day ahead and store covered at room temperature for best flavor.

faq.question: Can I use butter instead of vegetable oil?
faq.answer: You can substitute melted butter for vegetable oil in equal amounts, but it may change the texture slightly, making the cake a bit denser and less moist. Also, butter adds a richer flavor but requires cooling before mixing to avoid cooking the eggs (if you add any). For this recipe’s simplicity, oil works best.

faq.question: What happens if I skip the vinegar?
faq.answer: If you don’t have vinegar, you can substitute with an equal amount of lemon juice, but it may slightly alter the flavor.

faq.question: Is this cake vegan?
faq.answer: You can add vegan frosting or toppings to keep it completely plant-based.

faq.question: Can I make this cake gluten-free?
faq.answer: Make sure your baking powder or soda is gluten-free too.

faq.question: Why does my cake sometimes come out dry?
faq.answer: Removing the cake promptly when a toothpick test passes helps keep moisture in.

faq.question: Can I add chocolate chips or nuts?
faq.answer: Distribute mix-ins evenly for best results.

faq.question: How do I store leftovers?
faq.answer: Avoid leaving uncovered to prevent drying.

faq.question: Can I double this recipe?
faq.answer: Adjust oven racks if baking multiple pans.

conclusion: Give this simple chocolate cake a try—you’ll love how easy and comforting it is. Don’t forget to share your baking wins with me!
